front: Concept: the Stoic term for volition, the only thing fully within one's power, central to the philosophy of Epictetus
Work: compiled by Arrian, contains the injunction to 'behave in life as at a dinner party' and establishes three topoi one must follow
Philosopher: wrote moral letters to Lucilius, On the Shortness of Life, and On Mercy (dedicated to a ruler who later ordered his suicide)
Work: 12-book set of notes in which the author proposes 'either Providence or blind atoms' and reminds himself to 'erase' false impressions, partly written at a camp by the river Gran
Concept: Stoic doctrine that the cosmos is cyclically destroyed by fire at the end of each 'Great Year', influenced by Heraclitus's 'fire everliving'
work: Stoic Physics: Pneuma, Ekpyrosis, and Cosmology

front: Concept: a mixture of air and fire that infuses the universe as a vital organizing substance in Stoic cosmology
work: Stoic Physics: Pneuma, Ekpyrosis, and Cosmology

front: Philosopher: most prolific Stoic logician who died laughing after seeing a donkey eat figs; created five proposition connectives; introduced the 'growing argument' about Dion
Philosopher: described how a dog uses disjunctive syllogism to choose which path to take; used cone/cylinder analogy for determinism
Concept: Stoic truth-bearers described as 'sayables,' which the founder said 'were not somethings or qualified things, but rather quasi-somethings'
Philosopher: 16th-century Flemish author of On Constancy, sparked the development of neostoicism by synthesizing this ancient school with Christianity
Concept: Stoic analogy using a dog tied to a cart to illustrate how an autonomous will can be harmonized with determinism
